skill.name: nx-workspace-patterns
skill.description: Configure and optimize Nx monorepo workspaces with project structure, boundaries, caching, generators, and affected CI commands.
skill.summary: Apply production patterns for Nx monorepos, workspace boundaries, build caching, and affected command workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static command, filesystem, network, and weak-crypto findings are false positives from Markdown examples and normal Nx configuration references. No prompt injection, malicious execution path, or data exfiltration was found. A medium content risk remains because the remote cache template shows token placeholders inside configuration, which should be presented with clearer secret-management guidance.

medium_findings.title: Remote Cache Token Placement Guidance
medium_findings.description: The Nx Cloud cache example shows access token placeholders inside configuration. The values are not real secrets, but the template can encourage users to store credentials in committed project files instead of secrets management.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains explicit Nx Cloud token placeholders and a GitHub Actions secret reference. The example is legitimate documentation, but token placement needs human review and clearer secret-handling guidance.

low_findings.title: Documented Nx Commands Are Not Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command matches occur in Markdown inline text, code fences, shell examples, JSON examples, and TypeScript snippets. They document Nx usage and do not define an executable script, hook, or hidden command path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line context shows Markdown examples and configuration snippets rather than runnable skill code. No user-controlled command construction or automatic execution path is present.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links Are Expected Network References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to official Nx documentation, module boundary guidance, and Nx Cloud. They are reference links and not network calls made by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are Markdown resource links only. I found no request code, webhook endpoint, or instruction to send local data to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Nx Schema Parent Directory Reference Is Benign
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts refer to a project.json schema path that points from an app folder to node_modules. This is a normal Nx configuration pattern, not filesystem access by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The parent directory sequence appears inside a static JSON configuration example. There is no file read, write, deletion, archive extraction, or user input path join.

low_findings.title: Dotfile References Are Normal Nx Inputs
low_findings.description: The hidden file matches reference .eslintrc.json in Nx named input configuration. This is expected workspace metadata, not an attempt to access private files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both lines are static cache input patterns for ESLint configuration. They do not access secrets, SSH files, environment files, or user home directory dotfiles.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Match Has No Crypto Context
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not correspond to hashing, encryption, signing, or certificate code. The cited lines are frontmatter text and an ESLint overrides array.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line context contains no MD5, SHA1, DES, crypto API, or password handling. This is a scanner keyword false positive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Cache Token Placement Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The Nx Cloud cache example shows access token placeholders inside configuration. The values are not real secrets, but the template can encourage users to store credentials in committed project files instead of secrets management.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file contains explicit Nx Cloud token placeholders and a GitHub Actions secret reference. The example is legitimate documentation, but token placement needs human review and clearer secret-handling guidance.

content.value_statement: Large Nx monorepos are difficult to organize, cache, and validate consistently. This skill provides practical workspace patterns for structure, boundaries, generators, affected commands, and CI caching.
actual_capabilities: Explains Nx workspace organization for applications, libraries, and tooling projects.
Provides nx.json patterns for target defaults, named inputs, and cacheable operations.
Shows project.json examples for build, serve, test, and lint targets.
Defines module boundary rules with tags and dependency constraints.
Outlines affected-command CI workflows and remote caching configuration.
Includes a custom generator pattern for feature libraries.
limitations: It provides guidance and templates, but it does not execute Nx commands by itself.
Examples assume JavaScript or TypeScript Nx workspaces and may need adaptation.
It does not verify compatibility with the installed Nx version in a repository.
Remote cache credentials must be managed through secure secret storage.

use_cases.title: Standardize a growing monorepo
use_cases.description: Use the workspace structure, library types, and tagging patterns to keep applications and shared libraries maintainable.

use_cases.title: Improve CI performance
use_cases.description: Apply affected commands, cacheable targets, and remote cache patterns to reduce unnecessary builds and tests.

use_cases.title: Enforce project boundaries
use_cases.description: Use dependency constraints and consistent tags to prevent circular dependencies and uncontrolled cross-project imports.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an Nx workspace layout
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design an Nx workspace for these applications and shared libraries. Recommend folder structure, library types, and initial tags.

prompt_templates.title: Configure caching and target defaults
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Nx targets and suggest target defaults, named inputs, and cacheable operations for build, test, lint, and e2e.

prompt_templates.title: Design module boundary rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Create Nx module boundary rules for these scopes and library types. Explain which dependencies should be allowed or blocked.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize affected CI workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this CI workflow and convert it to use Nx affected commands, remote caching, parallelism, and safe secret handling.

output_examples.input: A team asks how to split a web app, API app, shared UI, and feature code in Nx.
output_examples.output: The response recommends apps for deployable units, focused libraries by type, consistent scope tags, and dependency boundaries.

output_examples.input: A CI pipeline runs every test and build for every pull request.
output_examples.output: The response proposes affected lint, test, build, and e2e steps, plus cache settings and parallelism guidance.

output_examples.input: A repository has uncontrolled imports between product areas.
output_examples.output: The response maps source tags to allowed dependency tags and explains how boundary rules reduce architecture drift.

best_practices: Start with clear project tags for type and scope before adding boundary rules.
Enable caching for stable build, lint, test, and e2e targets early in the workspace lifecycle.
Keep generated examples aligned with the repository package manager and installed Nx version.
anti_patterns: Do not store Nx Cloud access tokens directly in committed configuration files.
Do not create many small libraries without ownership, scope, or dependency rules.
Do not bypass affected commands in CI when the repository already has reliable project graph data.

faq.question: Does this skill run Nx commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and examples. A user or agent must decide when to run commands.

faq.question: Can it help with non-TypeScript Nx projects?
faq.answer: Partly. The architecture concepts apply broadly, but many templates use JavaScript and TypeScript tooling.

faq.question: Does it include module boundary guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes tag-based dependency constraints for application, feature, UI, data access, utility, and scope boundaries.

faq.question: Can it improve CI speed?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows affected commands, cacheable targets, parallelism, and remote cache configuration patterns.

faq.question: How should Nx Cloud tokens be handled?
faq.answer: Use environment variables or CI secret stores. Do not commit real access tokens to repository files.

faq.question: Does it replace Nx documentation?
faq.answer: No. It summarizes common production patterns and links to official Nx resources for detailed reference.
